content:
# Sorting and Searching

## What is it — Intuitive explanation

**Sorting** is the process of arranging data in a defined order (such as ascending or descending). **Searching** is the process of finding a specific piece of data within a collection. These two operations are often discussed together, and together they capture something very human: the idea of *organizing first, then looking things up*. As an algorithm, this mirrors what we do every day without thinking.

Imagine a bookshelf. Finding a single book among randomly placed books is frustrating, but if the books are arranged alphabetically by title (sorting), you can locate the one you need in no time (searching). Computers apply exactly this same logic — sorting and searching are how they handle data efficiently.

---

## Why it matters — Its place in computer science

Sorting and searching are among the first topics to study when learning algorithms, and they are also among the most frequently encountered operations in real-world software. Database queries, file management, search engines, game leaderboards — behind all of these, sorting and searching algorithms are running constantly.

Studying these topics also builds the foundation for **algorithmic thinking**: specifically, how to estimate the cost of a computation in terms of time and space. Because the difference between a naïve approach and a smarter one becomes concretely visible through **Big O notation**, sorting and searching serve as an ideal bridge into the theory of computational complexity.

---

## The core ideas — Definitions and key results

### Searching algorithms

**Linear Search** is the simplest method: examine each element one by one, starting from the beginning.

$$T(n) = O(n)$$

With $n$ elements, up to $n$ comparisons may be needed in the worst case. Its advantage is that it works on any data, regardless of whether it is sorted.

**Binary Search** is a far more efficient method, but it requires a **sorted array**. It works by repeatedly comparing the target value against the middle element, cutting the search space in half each time.

$$T(n) = O(\log n)$$

Even with $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ elements, at most $\lceil \log_2 1{,}000{,}000 \rceil = 20$ comparisons are needed — a remarkable improvement.

### Sorting algorithms

**Bubble Sort** repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are out of order, gradually "bubbling" larger values toward the end of the array.

$$T(n) = O(n^2)$$

It is easy to understand, but becomes very slow as the amount of data grows.

**Merge Sort** works by repeatedly splitting the array in half down to individual elements, then merging the pieces back together in sorted order. It is a classic example of the **divide and conquer** strategy.

$$T(n) = O(n \log n)$$

It has been proven that $\Omega(n \log n)$ is the theoretical lower bound for any comparison-based sorting algorithm, and Merge Sort achieves this bound.

---

## Concrete examples

### Example 1: Everyday life — Looking up a word in a dictionary

Think about how you use a printed dictionary. The words are already sorted alphabetically. You open to roughly the middle, decide whether your target word comes before or after that point, then focus on the relevant half. Repeating this process gets you to the right page very quickly. This is exactly **binary search**. If the dictionary were in random order, you would have no choice but to check every page from the start — that would be **linear search**.

### Example 2: Sorting a hand of playing cards — Insertion Sort

When you are dealt cards in a card game, most people naturally pick up each new card and slide it into the correct position among the cards already in hand. This is the idea behind **Insertion Sort**.

```
Hand: [7, 3, 5]
→ Hold 7 (sorted: [7])
→ Pick up 3 → smaller than 7, so insert before → [3, 7]
→ Pick up 5 → larger than 3 and smaller than 7, so insert between → [3, 5, 7]
```

Insertion Sort is highly efficient for small datasets or data that is already nearly sorted, achieving a best-case time complexity of $O(n)$.

### Example 3: Searching for a name in a contacts app — how sorting speeds up searching

A smartphone contacts app may hold hundreds of names. Because the app stores them in alphabetical order, searching for someone whose name starts with "M" means the app never needs to look through names starting with "A" through "L." When you type characters into the search box, binary search or a similar technique instantly narrows down the candidates. Without sorting, searching through a few hundred contacts might be tolerable, but with hundreds of thousands of contacts it could take several seconds per query.

### Example 4: Partitioning around a pivot in Quick Sort

**Quick Sort** selects one element as a "pivot," places all elements smaller than the pivot to its left and all larger elements to its right, then recursively repeats this process on each side.

```
[5, 3, 8, 1, 4]  ← pivot = 5
→ Left: [3, 1, 4]  Right: [8]
→ Recursively sort left and right
→ [1, 3, 4, 5, 8]
```

The average time complexity is $O(n \log n)$, but if pivot selection is poor (for example, always picking the smallest or largest element), the worst-case complexity degrades to $O(n^2)$. In practice, techniques such as random pivot selection keep it fast, and Quick Sort is used in the standard libraries of many programming languages.

---

## Connections — Relationships to other concepts

**Prerequisite concepts:**

- **Array**: The most fundamental data structure that sorting and searching operate on. Bubble Sort and Binary Search both assume array-based storage.
- **Tree**: A binary search tree (BST) maintains a sorted structure at all times, enabling $O(\log n)$ search, insertion, and deletion. It is essentially the idea of sorting and searching extended to a tree structure.
- **Hash Table**: An alternative approach that achieves $O(1)$ average search time without sorting. Understanding "sort then binary search" alongside "hash table" as the two major searching strategies deepens your grasp of both.

**Topics this leads into:**

- **Computational complexity theory (Big O notation)**: Sorting provides a natural entry point into topics like the proof of the $\Omega(n \log n)$ lower bound for comparison-based sorting.
- **Divide and Conquer**: Merge Sort and Quick Sort are canonical examples of divide and conquer, and serve as a bridge to more advanced algorithm design patterns.
- **Heap**: Leads naturally to Heap Sort and to the use of priority queues for efficient data management.

---

## Applications in other fields

**Databases and web services:**
Databases use **indexes** to speed up queries, and internally these indexes are typically implemented as B-Trees — sorted tree structures. In environments such as e-commerce product searches or social media feed retrieval, where tens of thousands of queries run every second, the efficiency of sorting and searching has a direct impact on the user experience.

**Bioinformatics:**
Genome analysis requires searching for specific patterns within DNA sequences that can be billions of characters long. Variants of binary search and sorted data structures such as suffix arrays are used extensively here, making sorting and searching ideas directly applicable to cutting-edge biological research.

**Machine learning:**
The k-nearest neighbors (k-NN) algorithm classifies new data points by finding the $k$ training examples closest to them. For large-scale datasets, approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search methods are used, and the efficiency of the underlying search algorithm directly governs the trade-off between accuracy and speed.

terms.definition: The operation of rearranging data into a defined order, such as ascending or descending. Also important as a preprocessing step to enable efficient searching.
The operation of finding a target piece of data within a collection of data. Multiple algorithms exist, including linear search and binary search.
The simplest search method, which checks items one by one from the beginning. Its time complexity is O(n), and it can be used even when the data is not sorted.
A search method that narrows the search range by half at each step by comparing with the middle value of a sorted array. Its time complexity is O(log n).
A sorting algorithm that repeatedly compares and swaps adjacent elements to move larger values toward the end. Its time complexity is O(n²); it is easy to understand but inefficient.
A sorting algorithm based on the divide and conquer approach that recursively splits an array in half and merges the halves back together in sorted order. Its time complexity is O(n log n).
A sorting algorithm that takes elements from the unsorted portion one at a time and inserts each into the correct position within the sorted portion. Efficient for data that is nearly sorted.
A sorting algorithm that recursively selects a pivot (reference value) and partitions the array into values smaller and larger than the pivot. Its average time complexity is O(n log n).
The reference value used to partition an array in Quick Sort. Values smaller than the pivot are placed to the left, and values larger are placed to the right.
An algorithm design pattern that recursively breaks a problem into smaller subproblems, solves them, and combines the results. It is the foundational idea behind Merge Sort and Quick Sort.

A notation that expresses the computational complexity (time and space cost) of an algorithm as a function of the number of data items n. Used to compare efficiency using expressions such as O(n), O(log n), and O(n²).
A data structure that maintains a sorted state using a tree structure, enabling search, insertion, and deletion in O(log n). It extends the concepts of sorting and searching to a tree.
A data structure that stores and retrieves data by transforming keys with a hash function. Achieves average O(1) search performance without the need for sorting.

diagrams.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 520" width="600" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="system-ui, sans-serif">

  <!-- 背景なし（透明） -->

  <!-- タイトル -->
  <text x="300" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Sorting and Searching — Concept Map</text>

  <!-- ===== 中央：ソート＆探索 ===== -->
  <ellipse cx="300" cy="130" rx="80" ry="32" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.1" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="300" y="125" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Sorting & Searching</text>
  <text x="300" y="143" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Organize and find</text>

  <!-- ===== 前提知識エリア（左上） ===== -->
  <rect x="20" y="60" width="110" height="38" rx="8" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="75" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Array</text>
  <text x="75" y="93" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Foundation of operations</text>

  <rect x="20" y="115" width="110" height="38" rx="8" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="75" y="135" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Tree</text>
  <text x="75" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Binary Search Tree BST</text>

  <rect x="20" y="170" width="110" height="38" rx="8" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="75" y="190" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Hash Table</text>
  <text x="75" y="203" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">O(1) search</text>

  <!-- 前提知識 → 中央 矢印 -->
  <line x1="130" y1="79" x2="218" y2="118" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.3" stroke-dasharray="4,3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="134" x2="218" y2="130" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.3" stroke-dasharray="4,3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <line x1="130" y1="189" x2="218" y2="142" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.3" stroke-dasharray="4,3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>

  <!-- 前提知識ラベル -->
  <text x="20" y="55" font-size="9" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.7">《Prerequisites》</text>

  <!-- ===== ソートアルゴリズム（左下） ===== -->
  <rect x="10" y="265" width="175" height="175" rx="10" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.06" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.8"/>
  <text x="97" y="283" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Sorting Algorithms</text>

  <!-- バブルソート -->
  <rect x="22" y="292" width="151" height="34" rx="6" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="97" y="307" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Bubble Sort</text>
  <text x="97" y="320" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Compare & swap neighbors  O(n²)</text>

  <!-- 挿入ソート -->
  <rect x="22" y="333" width="151" height="34" rx="6" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="97" y="348" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Insertion Sort</text>
  <text x="97" y="361" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Insert at correct position  O(n)~O(n²)</text>

  <!-- マージソート -->
  <rect x="22" y="374" width="151" height="34" rx="6" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="97" y="389" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Merge Sort</text>
  <text x="97" y="402" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Divide and conquer  O(n log n)</text>

  <!-- クイックソート -->
  <rect x="22" y="415" width="151" height="34" rx="6" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="97" y="430" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Quick Sort</text>
  <text x="97" y="443" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Pivot partition  O(n log n) avg</text>

  <!-- ===== 探索アルゴリズム（右下） ===== -->
  <rect x="415" y="265" width="170" height="120" rx="10" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.06" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.8"/>
  <text x="500" y="283" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#22c55e">Search Algorithms</text>

  <!-- 線形探索 -->
  <rect x="427" y="292" width="146" height="40" rx="6" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="500" y="307" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#22c55e">Linear Search</text>
  <text x="500" y="320" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Scan from start  O(n)</text>
  <text x="500" y="330" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">No sorting needed</text>

  <!-- 二分探索 -->
  <rect x="427" y="339" width="146" height="40" rx="6" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="500" y="354" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="#22c55e">Binary Search</text>
  <text x="500" y="367" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Compare middle & halve  O(log n)</text>
  <text x="500" y="377" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">Sorted input required</text>

  <!-- ===== ソート済みの前提リンク ===== -->
  <line x1="185" y1="390" x2="415" y2="354" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1.8" stroke-dasharray="6,3" marker-end="url(#arrRed)"/>
  <text x="300" y="363" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">Provides sorted data</text>

  <!-- ===== 発展先（右上） ===== -->
  <rect x="465" y="60" width="120" height="38" rx="8" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="525" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Complexity Theory</text>
  <text x="525" y="93" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Big O notation</text>

  <rect x="465" y="115" width="120" height="38" rx="8" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="525" y="135" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Divide & Conquer</text>
  <text x="525" y="148" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">D&C pattern</text>

  <rect x="465" y="170" width="120" height="38" rx="8" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2"/>
  <text x="525" y="190" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Heap</text>
  <text x="525" y="203" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">Priority queue</text>

  <!-- 中央 → 発展先 矢印 -->
  <line x1="382" y1="118" x2="463" y2="79" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <line x1="382" y1="130" x2="463" y2="134" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>
  <line x1="382" y1="142" x2="463" y2="189" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.3" marker-end="url(#arr)"/>

  <text x="580" y="55" font-size="9" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.7" text-anchor="end">《Advanced Topics》</text>

  <!-- ===== 中央 → ソート/探索 矢印 ===== -->
  <line x1="240" y1="155" x2="155" y2="265" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#arrBlue)"/>
  <line x1="360" y1="155" x2="450" y2="265" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.6" marker-end="url(#arrGreen)"/>

  <!-- ===== 計算量比較バー（下部） ===== -->
  <rect x="185" y="400" width="225" height="95" rx="8" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3,3"/>
  <text x="297" y="416" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Complexity Comparison (n=1 million)</text>

  <!-- O(n^2) -->
  <text x="195" y="433" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">O(n²)</text>
  <rect x="235" y="423" width="100" height="8" rx="2" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.7"/>
  <text x="340" y="432" font-size="8" fill="#ef4444">1 trillion ops</text>

  <!-- O(n log n) -->
  <text x="195" y="452" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">O(n log n)</text>
  <rect x="235" y="442" width="40" height="8" rx="2" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.7"/>
  <text x="280" y="451" font-size="8" fill="#3b82f6">20 million ops</text>

  <!-- O(n) -->
  <text x="195" y="471" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">O(n)</text>
  <rect x="235" y="461" width="16" height="8" rx="2" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.7"/>
  <text x="256" y="470" font-size="8" fill="#22c55e">1 million ops</text>

  <!-- O(log n) -->
  <text x="195" y="490" font-size="9" fill="currentColor">O(log n)</text>
  <rect x="235" y="480" width="3" height="8" rx="1" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.9"/>
  <text x="242" y="489" font-size="8" fill="#22c55e">~20 ops!</text>

  <!-- ===== 矢印マーカー定義 ===== -->
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="currentColor"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrBlue" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#3b82f6"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrGreen" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#22c55e"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrRed" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#ef4444"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

</svg>

quiz.question: Which of the following is a required condition for using Binary Search?

choices.text: The number of data elements must be even
The array to be searched must be sorted
The first element of the array must be the maximum value

quiz.explanation: Binary Search is a technique that narrows the search range in half by comparing the middle value with the target value. For this operation to work, the array must be sorted in ascending or descending order. The data type and whether the number of elements is odd or even are not relevant conditions.

quiz.question: Which of the following best describes why the $O(n \log n)$ time complexity of Merge Sort is particularly significant?

choices.text: Because the theoretical lower bound of time complexity for comparison-based sorting algorithms is $O(n \log n)$, and Merge Sort achieves that limit
Because it can deliver performance close to $O(n)$ for any kind of data
Because it has been proven to have the same time complexity as searches using a hash table
Because it is the only sorting algorithm that uses the divide-and-conquer approach

quiz.explanation: It has been proven that a theoretical lower bound of $O(n \log n)$ exists for comparison-based sorting algorithms. Merge Sort achieves exactly this lower bound, making it one of the theoretically optimal algorithms in terms of efficiency for comparison-based sorting.

quiz.question: Which of the following correctly compares linear search and binary search?

choices.text: Linear search can only be used on sorted arrays, while binary search can be used on any array
Binary search is always faster than linear search, so there is no situation where linear search should be used
Linear search can be used on unsorted data, whereas binary search requires sorted data and offers a higher search efficiency of $O(\log n)$
Binary search has a time complexity of $O(n)$, which is slower than linear search’s $O(\log n)$

quiz.explanation: Linear search examines data sequentially from the beginning at $O(n)$, so it can be applied to unsorted arrays. Binary search, on the other hand, requires a sorted array and narrows the search range in half each time, achieving $O(\log n)$ speed, but cannot be used on unsorted data. Each has its own appropriate use case.
